_comment: Curated constellation lore for Hypatia (Celestarium). Hand-written; NOT produced by dev_build_hypatia_catalog.py and never overwritten by it. Keys are IAU abbreviations. Myth and naming origin only — no observing data. Edit freely; this file is config, committed to the repo.

lore.And: Andromeda, the chained princess. Her mother Cassiopeia's boasting angered the sea nymphs, and Andromeda was offered to the monster Cetus in payment — until Perseus, flying home with the Gorgon's head, turned the beast to stone and claimed her.
lore.Ant: The Air Pump. One of Lacaille's 18th-century southern inventions, honoring the vacuum pump of physicist Denis Papin. No myth here — the southern sky was charted in the age of instruments, and Lacaille filled it with the tools of science.
lore.Aps: The Bird-of-Paradise. Named by Dutch navigators in the 1590s after the spectacular New Guinea birds, whose trade skins arrived in Europe with the feet removed — 'apus' means footless, and the legend of a bird that never lands stuck.
lore.Aqr: The Water-Bearer. Usually identified with Ganymede, the Trojan youth so beautiful that Zeus, in eagle form, carried him to Olympus to pour wine for the gods. One of the oldest constellations, tied to floods and rain across many ancient cultures.
lore.Aql: The Eagle. The bird of Zeus himself, who carried the god's thunderbolts — and, in one telling, carried Ganymede up to Olympus. Its bright heart is Altair, one point of the Summer Triangle.
lore.Ara: The Altar. Here the gods swore their oaths before the war against the Titans, and the smoke of the altar was said to rise as the Milky Way. After the victory, Zeus set the altar in the sky in gratitude.
lore.Ari: The Ram. The golden-fleeced ram that rescued Phrixus and Helle from sacrifice, flying them east across the sea. Helle fell into the strait that took her name; the fleece Phrixus hung in Colchis became the prize of Jason and the Argonauts.
lore.Aur: The Charioteer. Usually Erichthonius, the lame-born king of Athens who invented the four-horse chariot to carry himself, and was set in the sky for the achievement. He is drawn holding a she-goat — the star Capella — and her kids.
lore.Boo: The Herdsman. The ox-driver who follows the Great Bear around the pole, sometimes identified with Arcas, son of Zeus and Callisto — the very bear he pursues. His name survives in his brightest star, Arcturus, 'guardian of the bear'.
lore.Cae: The Chisel. Another of Lacaille's southern instruments — the engraver's burin, a tool of the workshop rather than the myth-hall. Faint and small, a monument to craft.
lore.Cam: The Giraffe. A 17th-century invention of Petrus Plancius filling the dim space near the pole. Some early charts tied it to the camel that carried Rebecca to Isaac, but the name that survived is the 'camel-leopard' — the giraffe.
lore.Cnc: The Crab. Sent by Hera to pinch Heracles' feet as he wrestled the Hydra. It was crushed underfoot almost at once — but Hera, loyal to even her smallest agent, placed it in the sky. Within it lies the Beehive cluster, known since antiquity.
lore.CVn: The Hunting Dogs. Asterion and Chara, the two hounds held on a leash by Boötes as he drives the Great Bear around the pole. Carved out of Ursa Major's shadow by Hevelius in the 17th century.
lore.CMa: The Great Dog. Laelaps, the hound destined to catch whatever it chased — or simply Orion's faithful companion, following the hunter across the sky. Its eye is Sirius, the brightest star in the night, whose rising marked the flooding of the Nile.
lore.CMi: The Little Dog. Orion's second hound, or Maera, the dog of Icarius, who led the man's daughter to his grave and was rewarded with the sky. Barely more than its bright star Procyon — 'before the dog' — which rises just ahead of Sirius.
lore.Cap: The Sea-Goat. Half goat, half fish — often the god Pan, who leapt into the Nile to escape the monster Typhon and changed only his lower half in time. One of the oldest figures in the sky, drawn this way since Babylon.
lore.Car: The Keel. The hull of the great ship Argo, which carried Jason and fifty heroes to Colchis for the Golden Fleece. The ancient ship-constellation was so vast that Lacaille broke it into keel, stern, and sails; the keel carries Canopus, the pilot's star.
lore.Cas: Cassiopeia, the vain queen. She boasted that she — or her daughter — outshone the sea nymphs, and doomed Andromeda to the monster. Chained to her throne in the sky, she circles the pole head-down half of every night as punishment.
lore.Cen: The Centaur. Chiron, wisest of the centaurs — tutor of Heracles, Achilles, and Asclepius. Wounded by a poisoned arrow and unable to die, he traded his immortality away and was honored with the sky. His forefoot is the nearest star system to our own.
lore.Cep: Cepheus, king of Aethiopia — husband of Cassiopeia, father of Andromeda. He stands beside his queen at the pole, the whole royal family of the Perseus legend gathered in one stretch of northern sky.
lore.Cet: The Sea Monster. The beast sent to devour Andromeda, turned to stone by Perseus with the Gorgon's head. Later ages softened it into a whale, but the old charts draw a proper monster. Its chest holds Mira, 'the wonderful', the first star seen to fade and return.
lore.Cha: The Chameleon. A southern invention of the Dutch navigators Keyser and de Houtman, named for the color-changing lizard of Madagascar. On old charts it is drawn with its tongue out, catching the neighboring constellation of the Fly.
lore.Cir: The Drawing Compasses. Lacaille's dividers, placed fittingly beside the Centaur's pointer stars. A draftsman's tool for a mapped sky.
lore.Col: The Dove. Named for the dove Noah sent from the Ark — and, in classical dress, the dove the Argonauts released to test the Clashing Rocks. It flies just ahead of the old ship Argo in the sky.
lore.Com: Berenice's Hair. Queen Berenice II of Egypt vowed her hair to the gods for her husband's safe return from war. The shorn locks vanished from the temple, and the court astronomer announced they had been placed among the stars — the only constellation named for a real person's real hair.
lore.CrA: The Southern Crown. A wreath rather than a royal crown — sometimes the crown of laurel that slipped from the head of the centaur Sagittarius, who wears it at his feet.
lore.CrB: The Northern Crown. The jeweled crown of Ariadne, given at her wedding to Dionysus after Theseus abandoned her on Naxos. The god threw it into the sky so her glory would outlast the marriage.
lore.Crv: The Crow. Apollo's bird, sent to fetch water in a cup. It dallied waiting for figs to ripen, then blamed a water snake for the delay. Apollo saw the lie and fixed all three — crow, cup, and snake — in the sky, the crow forever thirsty beside a cup it cannot reach.
lore.Crt: The Cup. The goblet from the tale of Apollo's crow, set just out of the lying bird's reach on the back of Hydra. Sometimes also called the cup of Dionysus.
lore.Cru: The Southern Cross. The smallest constellation, known to the ancients as part of the Centaur but lost to northern skies by precession. Rediscovered by European navigators, it became the great signpost of the southern hemisphere and marks the flags of nations beneath it.
lore.Cyg: The Swan. Zeus in disguise, flying to Leda — or Orpheus, placed near his lyre after death, or the grieving Cycnus searching a river for his friend Phaethon's body. It flies down the Milky Way, and its tail star Deneb anchors the Summer Triangle.
lore.Del: The Dolphin. The messenger that persuaded the sea nymph Amphitrite to marry Poseidon — or the dolphin that carried the musician Arion safely to shore after pirates forced him overboard. A small, distinct diamond of stars, beloved of sailors.
lore.Dor: The Goldfish — properly the dolphinfish or mahi-mahi seen by the Dutch navigators in tropical seas. It swims through the far south carrying most of the Large Magellanic Cloud on its back.
lore.Dra: The Dragon. Ladon, the hundred-headed serpent that guarded the golden apples of the Hesperides and never slept, slain by Heracles and coiled by Hera around the pole of the sky. Its star Thuban was the pole star when the pyramids were raised.
lore.Equ: The Little Horse. A foal beside Pegasus — sometimes Celeris, brother or offspring of the winged horse, given by Hermes. The second-smallest constellation, ancient despite its faintness.
lore.Eri: The River. The stream into which Phaethon fell, scorched from the sky after losing control of the sun-chariot; his sisters wept amber on its banks. It winds from Orion's foot deep into the southern sky, ending at bright Achernar — 'the river's end'.
lore.For: The Furnace. Lacaille's chemical furnace, honoring the chemistry of his age — later linked to Lavoisier. It sits in a bend of the celestial river Eridanus.
lore.Gem: The Twins. Castor and Polydeuces (Pollux), brothers of Helen — one mortal, one divine. When Castor was killed, Pollux begged to share his immortality, and Zeus set them together in the sky. They were the sailors' protectors, invoked in storms.
lore.Gru: The Crane. Named by the Dutch navigators for the long-necked bird, a symbol of watchfulness in many traditions. It stands below the Southern Fish in the deep south.
lore.Her: Heracles, the kneeling hero. The ancients drew him simply as 'the Kneeler', a mystery figure on one knee; later ages recognized the greatest of heroes, mid-labor, with his foot on the Dragon's head. His twelve labors gave the sky several of its beasts.
lore.Hor: The Pendulum Clock. Lacaille's tribute to Christiaan Huygens' great invention, the instrument that gave astronomy precise time. On old charts the pendulum swings among the southern stars.
lore.Hya: The Water Snake. The many-headed Hydra of Lerna, slain by Heracles — each severed head grew back double until fire sealed the necks. Fittingly, it is the largest constellation, more than a quarter of the way around the sky, though only one bright star, its heart Alphard, stands alone.
lore.Hyi: The Little Water Snake. A companion serpent added by the Dutch navigators, coiled near the south pole between the Magellanic Clouds — a modern echo of the ancient Hydra, with no myth of its own.
lore.Ind: The Indian. Named by the Dutch navigators for the indigenous peoples met on their voyages — drawn holding arrows and spears. A 16th-century sailor's constellation, product of the age of exploration.
lore.Lac: The Lizard. One of Hevelius' 17th-century inventions, slipped into a narrow dim gap between the Swan and Andromeda. He remarked that only a small nimble creature could fit the space.
lore.Leo: The Lion. The Nemean Lion, whose hide no weapon could pierce; Heracles strangled it as his first labor and wore its pelt ever after. One of the oldest constellations — the Mesopotamians saw a lion here millennia before Greece — with royal Regulus at its heart.
lore.LMi: The Little Lion. A cub added by Hevelius in the blank space between the Lion and the Great Bear. No myth attends it; it simply keeps the great cat company.
lore.Lep: The Hare. Crouched forever at the feet of Orion, hunted by his dogs — placed there, one story says, by Hermes for its swiftness. Another tale makes it a warning: an island overrun by hares set the figure in the sky as a lesson in excess.
lore.Lib: The Scales. The only zodiac figure that is an object, not a creature. To the Greeks these stars were the Scorpion's claws; Rome made them the scales of justice, held by neighboring Virgo as the goddess Astraea. Its star names still mean 'northern claw' and 'southern claw'.
lore.Lup: The Wolf. To the ancients simply 'the Beast', impaled on the Centaur's spear as an offering carried toward the Altar. Later tradition named it a wolf — sometimes Lycaon, the king turned to one by Zeus for serving human flesh.
lore.Lyn: The Lynx. Hevelius named this faint region honestly: he said you would need the eyes of a lynx to see it. There is no myth — only an astronomer's dry joke that stuck for three centuries.
lore.Lyr: The Lyre. The instrument of Orpheus, whose music charmed stones, beasts, and finally Hades himself — though he lost Eurydice at the threshold of the living world. After his death the Muses set the lyre in the sky, marked by brilliant Vega.
lore.Men: The Table Mountain. The only constellation named for a real place on Earth: Table Mountain above the Cape of Good Hope, where Lacaille made his southern observations. The Large Magellanic Cloud hangs above it like the mountain's famous tablecloth of cloud.
lore.Mic: The Microscope. Lacaille's tribute to the instrument that opened the small world, as the telescope opened the large. A faint rectangle south of Capricornus.
lore.Mon: The Unicorn. A 17th-century addition attributed to Plancius, filling the space inside the Winter Triangle. The unicorn of medieval legend — pure, elusive, and untameable — walking the Milky Way between Orion's dogs.
lore.Mus: The Fly. The Dutch navigators' southern bee — later charts renamed it the fly — buzzing just below the Southern Cross, prey for the nearby Chameleon's tongue.
lore.Nor: The Set Square. Lacaille's carpenter's square and rule, placed in the Milky Way beside the Compasses and the Southern Triangle — a small district of the sky given over entirely to geometry.
lore.Oct: The Octant. The navigational instrument of John Hadley, forerunner of the sextant — fittingly, this faint figure contains the south celestial pole itself, the point every southern navigator must find.
lore.Oph: The Serpent-Bearer. Asclepius, the healer taught by Chiron, who grew so skilled he raised the dead. Hades complained, Zeus struck him down — and then honored him among the stars, gripping the serpent whose shed skin taught him the secret of renewal.
lore.Ori: Orion, the great hunter. A giant who boasted he would slay every beast on Earth, felled by the Scorpion sent to humble him — the two were placed on opposite sides of the sky, never visible together. His belt of three stars is the most recognized signpost in the heavens.
lore.Pav: The Peacock. The bird of Hera, whose tail she set with the hundred eyes of the slain watchman Argus. The Dutch navigators, likely thinking of the Java peacock, gave the far south its own.
lore.Peg: The Winged Horse. Pegasus sprang from the blood of Medusa when Perseus struck off her head. He carried the hero Bellerophon against the Chimera — and threw him when he presumed to fly to Olympus. The horse flew on alone and was given the sky.
lore.Per: Perseus, the hero. Slayer of the Gorgon Medusa, whose gaze turned men to stone; rescuer of Andromeda from the sea monster. He stands in the sky holding the severed head, marked by the star Algol — the 'demon's head' that visibly winks.
lore.Phe: The Phoenix. The bird that lives for centuries, burns on its own pyre, and rises renewed from the ashes. The Dutch navigators set the emblem of rebirth at the head of the celestial river in the southern sky.
lore.Pic: The Painter's Easel. Lacaille's easel and palette, completing his gallery of arts and instruments. It stands beside brilliant Canopus in the far south.
lore.Psc: The Fishes. Aphrodite and her son Eros, fleeing the monster Typhon, plunged into the Euphrates and became two fish — tied together with a cord so they would not lose each other. The knot star, Alrescha, still binds the pair.
lore.PsA: The Southern Fish. The parent of the zodiac's Fishes, and older than both — a Babylonian figure that drinks the stream poured from Aquarius' jar. Its solitary bright star is Fomalhaut, 'the mouth of the fish'.
lore.Pup: The Stern. The poop deck of the ship Argo, the largest piece left when Lacaille dismantled the ancient vessel. The ship of Jason's quest still sails the southern Milky Way in three parts.
lore.Pyx: The Compass. The mariner's magnetic compass — Lacaille placed it where Argo's mast might stand, giving the old ship a modern instrument its crew never had.
lore.Ret: The Reticle. Named for the crosshair grid in Lacaille's telescope eyepiece — the tiny net of lines through which he measured ten thousand southern stars. A constellation honoring the act of star-charting itself.
lore.Sge: The Arrow. A shaft flying down the Milky Way — the arrow of Heracles that slew the eagle tormenting Prometheus, or a dart of Eros. The third-smallest constellation, but recognized since antiquity.
lore.Sgr: The Archer. A centaur drawing his bow — often the gentle Crotus, inventor of archery and companion of the Muses, rather than a warlike beast. His arrow aims at the Scorpion's heart, and behind his teapot-shaped stars lies the center of our galaxy.
lore.Sco: The Scorpion. The creature sent to sting the boastful hunter Orion — killer and victim placed at opposite ends of the sky, so that Orion sets as the Scorpion rises. Its red heart is Antares, 'rival of Mars'. One of the few constellations that looks like its name.
lore.Scl: The Sculptor's Studio. Lacaille's workshop of the arts — originally 'the sculptor's apparatus', a carved head on a block with mallet and chisels. Shortened by later astronomers to simply the Sculptor.
lore.Sct: The Shield. The only constellation honoring a then-living person: Hevelius named it Sobieski's Shield for King Jan III Sobieski of Poland, who broke the siege of Vienna in 1683 — and who had supported the astronomer after fire destroyed his observatory.
lore.Ser: The Serpent. The snake gripped by Ophiuchus, and the only constellation split in two — its head (Caput) rises on one side of the healer, its tail (Cauda) on the other. Serpents shed their skin and renew; in Asclepius' hands, the emblem of medicine.
lore.Sex: The Sextant. Hevelius' memorial to his own great sextant, the instrument he used for decades to measure star positions by naked eye — and which burned with his observatory in 1679. He set it in the sky between the Lion and the Water Snake.
lore.Tau: The Bull. Zeus in the form of a magnificent white bull, who carried Europa across the sea to Crete — only head and forequarters show above the waves. Its red eye is Aldebaran; its shoulder carries the Pleiades, the seven sisters pursued by Orion.
lore.Tel: The Telescope. Lacaille's homage to the instrument that transformed astronomy. On his chart it was a grand aerial telescope on a mast; the modern boundaries keep only a modest strip of it.
lore.Tri: The Triangle. Recognized since the Greeks, who saw the letter delta or the delta of the Nile — some said the island of Sicily, beloved of Demeter. Small, ancient, and exactly what it says.
lore.TrA: The Southern Triangle. The southern counterpart set by the Dutch navigators — sometimes drawn as a surveyor's level, three points of reference in the far southern sky near the Pointers.
lore.Tuc: The Toucan. The great-billed bird of the Americas, named by the Dutch navigators. It shelters the Small Magellanic Cloud and one of the sky's finest globular clusters beneath its wing.
lore.UMa: The Great Bear. Callisto, companion of Artemis, loved by Zeus and transformed into a bear by a jealous goddess. Zeus swung her into the sky by the tail — stretching it long — where she circles the pole, never setting, never resting. Her seven brightest stars form the Plough, or Big Dipper.
lore.UMi: The Little Bear. Arcas, son of Callisto, placed beside his mother — or one of the nymphs who nursed the infant Zeus in hiding. At the tip of its stretched tail stands Polaris, the pole star, the still point around which the whole northern sky turns.
lore.Vel: The Sails. The canvas of the ship Argo, billowing in the southern Milky Way between the keel and the stern. When Lacaille divided the great ship, the sails kept some of its brightest stars.
lore.Vir: The Maiden. Demeter of the harvest, or her daughter Persephone — or Astraea, goddess of justice, last of the immortals to abandon the Earth at the end of the Golden Age, who holds the neighboring Scales. Her hand carries Spica, the ear of wheat.
lore.Vol: The Flying Fish. Named by the Dutch navigators for the fish they watched glide over tropical waves — drawn on old charts leaping ahead of the ship Argo, as flying fish run before a real hull, with the Goldfish in pursuit.
lore.Vul: The Little Fox. Hevelius drew a fox carrying a goose to the nearby Eagle and Vulture — 'the fox with the goose'. The goose was later dropped from the name, but its star keeps the memory: Anser, the goose, is the fox's brightest.
